title: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes
authors: Steven M. Holland, John I. Gallin $ ^{*} $

items.title: Understanding Benign Ethnic Neutropenia in African Americans
items.content: Benign ethnic neutropenia is a condition observed in certain African-American populations characterized by lower granulocyte counts, typically ranging from 1500–2000/μL. This variation does not usually result in clinical sequelae and is associated with the null expression of the Duffy antigen receptor for cytokines (DARC) gene.
keyPoints: Benign ethnic neutropenia involves granulocyte numbers often in the range of 1500–2000/μL.
This condition is generally without clinical consequences.
It is associated with null expression of the Duffy antigen receptor for cytokines (DARC) gene, providing resistance to Plasmodium vivax malaria.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes

items.title: Normal Leukocyte Counts and Their Clinical Significance
items.content: Understanding normal leukocyte counts is crucial for identifying deviations that may indicate disease. Normal blood leukocyte counts range from 4.3–10.8 × 10⁹/L, with specific percentages attributed to different cell types: neutrophils (45–74%), bands (0–4%), lymphocytes (16–45%), monocytes (4–10%), eosinophils (0–7%), and basophils (0–2%).
keyPoints: Normal blood leukocyte count ranges from 4.3–10.8 × 10⁹/L.
Neutrophils typically represent 45–74% of total leukocytes.
Lymphocytes account for 16–45%, monocytes for 4–10%, eosinophils for 0–7%, and basophils for 0–2%.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes

items.title: Neutrophil Production and Mobilization
items.content: In normal humans, neutrophils are produced only in the bone marrow with a minimum of 400–500 stem cells required to support hematopoiesis. The system produces approximately $1.3 \times 10^{11}$ neutrophils per day for an 80-kg person and has a reserve that can be mobilized during inflammation or infection.
keyPoints: Neutrophils are produced only in the bone marrow.
Minimum stem cells required: 400–500.
Daily production of neutrophils: $1.3 \times 10^{11}$ per 80-kg person.
Reserve can be mobilized during inflammation or infection.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MATURATION

items.title: Neutrophil Maturation and Morphology
items.content: Neutrophils mature from pluripotent stem cells over approximately two weeks. The maturation process includes the formation of primary (azurophil) granules in promyelocytes, secondary granules in myelocytes, and a transition through metamyelocyte to band neutrophil with a sausage-shaped nucleus. Mature neutrophils have up to four nuclear segments.
keyPoints: Maturation takes about two weeks: 1 week for proliferation (to metamyelocyte), 1 week for maturation (to mature neutrophil).
Primary granules in promyelocytes contain hydrolases, elastase, myeloperoxidase, cathepsin G, cationic proteins.
Secondary granules in myelocytes include lactoferrin, vitamin B12-binding protein, NOX2 components.
Mature neutrophils have up to four nuclear segments.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MATURATION

items.title: Neutrophil Morphological Abnormalities and Clinical Implications
items.content: Excessive segmentation (>5 lobes) in neutrophils may indicate folate or vitamin B12 deficiency, or WHIM syndrome. The Pelger-Huet anomaly results in bilobed nuclei due to mutations in the lamin B receptor, while pseudo-Pelger-Huet can occur with infections or myelodysplastic syndromes.
keyPoints: Excessive segmentation (>5 lobes) suggests folate/vitamin B12 deficiency or WHIM syndrome.
Pelger-Huet anomaly: bilobed nuclei due to lamin B receptor mutations.
Pseudo-Pelger-Huet anomaly can occur with infections or myelodysplastic syndromes.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MATURATION

items.title: Understanding Neutrophil Margination
items.content: Neutrophils are marginated in the circulation through interactions with endothelial cells, primarily mediated by selectins. In pulmonary capillaries, neutrophil size and deformability facilitate margination, while in systemic post-capillary venules, selectin-mediated rolling occurs.
keyPoints: Under normal conditions, ~90% of neutrophils are in the bone marrow, 2–3% in circulation.
Margination involves L-selectin on neutrophils binding to glycosylated proteins like GlyCAM1 and CD34 on endothelial cells.
In systemic venules, selectins (E-selectin [CD62E] and P-selectin [CD62P]) mediate rolling of neutrophils along the endothelium.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms of Neutrophil Adhesion and Diapedesis
items.content: Neutrophil adhesion to the endothelium is enhanced by chemotactic stimuli, leading to increased expression of integrins. This facilitates diapedesis, where neutrophils migrate through endothelial junctions into tissues.
keyPoints: Chemotactic stimuli (e.g., C5a, IL-8) increase neutrophil adhesiveness via intracellular adhesion proteins.
Integrins like CD11a/CD18 and CD11b/CD18 bind to endothelial ICAM1 and 2.
Diapedesis involves PECAM-1 (CD31), allowing neutrophils to migrate through endothelial junctions.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 1)

items.title: Neutrophil Lifecycle and Clearance
items.content: Neutrophils have a short circulation half-life of 6–7 hours, after which they are cleared by macrophages in the lung and spleen. They migrate through mucous membranes for clearance.
keyPoints: Normal neutrophil half-life is 6–7 hours.
Senescent neutrophils are cleared by macrophages in the lung and spleen.
Most neutrophils exit the body via migration through oral and gastrointestinal mucosa.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms of Neutrophil-Mediated Microbial Killing
items.content: Neutrophils utilize a series of biochemical reactions to kill microorganisms. Upon activation, neutrophils assemble NADPH oxidase, which reduces oxygen to superoxide anion. This is converted into hydrogen peroxide and other reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as hypochlorous acid, through the action of myeloperoxidase. These ROS are potent antimicrobial agents that can also damage host tissues if uncontrolled.
keyPoints: NADPH oxidase catalyzes reduction of oxygen to superoxide anion.
Superoxide dismutase converts superoxide anion into hydrogen peroxide and other toxic oxygen products.
Hydrogen peroxide + chloride + myeloperoxidase generates hypochlorous acid (bleach), which is a potent antimicrobial agent.
Strongly cationic proteins, defensins, elastase, cathepsins, and nitric oxide participate in microbial killing.
Lactoferrin chelates iron, inhibiting microorganism growth.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 2)

items.title: Role of Chemokines in Inflammation and Immune Response
items.content: Chemokines are small proteins that regulate the recruitment and activation of various immune cells, including neutrophils, monocytes, eosinophils, and lymphocytes. They signal through G protein-linked receptors with seven transmembrane domains. Chemokines are categorized into four groups based on cysteine structure: C, CC, CXC, and CXXXC. Each group has specific roles in attracting different immune cells and can also influence viral infections and other conditions.
keyPoints: Chemokines regulate recruitment and activation of neutrophils, monocytes, eosinophils, and lymphocytes.
Signal transduction occurs through G protein-linked receptors with seven cell membrane-spanning domains.
C chemokine (lymphotactin) is T-cell tropic; CC chemokines attract lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, and basophils.
CXC cytokines like IL-8 mainly attract neutrophils.
CXXXC chemokine fractalkine attracts neutrophils, monocytes, and T cells.
Chemokine receptors serve as co-receptors for HIV infection and play roles in other viral infections.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 2)

items.title: Impact of Neutrophil Count on Infection Risk
items.content: Neutropenia significantly increases susceptibility to infections. The risk escalates sharply when neutrophil counts drop below certain thresholds, affecting the body's ability to control microbial flora and local inflammatory responses.
keyPoints: Infections become severe when neutrophil counts fall to <1000 cells/μL.
Impaired control of endogenous microbial flora occurs at ANC <500 cells/μL.
Absence of local inflammatory response is noted when ANC falls below 200/μL.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.title: Causes and Management of Iatrogenic Neutropenia
items.content: Iatrogenic neutropenia is commonly caused by cytotoxic or immunosuppressive therapies, certain antibiotics, and drugs acting as immune haptens. Discontinuation of the offending drug typically leads to recovery.
keyPoints: Cytotoxic or immunosuppressive therapies are common causes due to decreased progenitor cell production.
Antibiotics like chloramphenicol and sulfa-containing compounds can inhibit myeloid precursor proliferation.
Drug-induced neutropenia often resolves within 5–7 days after discontinuation of the sensitizing drug.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.title: Autoimmune and Cyclic Neutropenias
items.content: Acquired neutropenia can be autoimmune or cyclic, often associated with large granular lymphocytosis. Management may involve immunosuppressive therapies.
keyPoints: Circulating antineutrophil antibodies cause increased destruction of neutrophils in autoimmune neutropenias.
Viral infections like HIV and cyclic patterns can lead to acquired neutropenia.
Management includes glucocorticoids, cyclosporine, methotrexate, and monoclonal antibodies.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.title: Hereditary Neutropenia and Associated Syndromes
items.content: Hereditary neutropenia encompasses various congenital forms, each linked to specific genetic mutations. These conditions manifest as profound constant neutropenia or agranulocytosis and can be fatal if untreated.
keyPoints: Kostmann's syndrome: Neutrophil count <100/μL due to HAX-1 gene mutation.
Severe chronic neutropenia: Neutrophil count of 300–1500/μL linked to ELANE mutations.
Cyclic neutropenia/cyclic hematopoiesis: Due to ELANE mutations.
Cartilage-hair hypoplasia syndrome: Linked to RMRP gene mutation.
Shwachman-Diamond syndrome: Associated with pancreatic insufficiency and SBDs gene mutation.
WHIM syndrome: Characterized by neutrophil hypersegmentation, bone marrow myeloid arrest due to CXCR4 mutations.
G-CSF receptor mutations in severe congenital neutropenia can lead to leukemia development.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.title: Felty Syndrome and Neutrophilia
items.content: Felty syndrome involves a triad of rheumatoid arthritis, splenomegaly, and neutropenia. It can be managed by addressing antibodies that shorten neutrophil lifespan or attacking marrow precursors.
keyPoints: Antibodies in Felty syndrome can reduce neutrophil lifespan.
Large granular lymphocytes may attack marrow neutrophil precursors.
Splenectomy might increase neutrophil count and lower serum neutrophil-binding IgG.
Neutrophilia from acute infection involves increased production and release, with cell counts of 10,000–25,000/μL.
Leukemoid reaction: Persistent neutrophilia with ≥30,000–50,000/μL, distinguished by mature circulating neutrophils.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.title: Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency
items.content: Leukocyte adhesion deficiency (LAD) is characterized by impaired neutrophil exit from circulation, leading to leukocytosis and increased infection risk.
keyPoints: LAD types are autosomal recessive disorders impairing neutrophil migration.
LAD 1: Caused by mutations in CD18 affecting integrins like LFA-1 and Mac-1.
Defective adhesion leads to poor neutrophil adherence, spreading, aggregation, and chemotaxis.
Severe phenotype includes lack of leukocyte integrin expression on neutrophils and immune cells.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiencies and Their Clinical Implications
items.content: Leukocyte adhesion deficiencies (LADs) are characterized by defects in neutrophil function, leading to recurrent infections. LAD 1 involves a defect in integrin activation due to mutations in ITGB2 or FERMT3, causing severe bacterial infections and persistent leukocytosis with resting neutrophil counts of 15,000–20,000/μL. LAD 2 results from abnormal fucosylation of SLe^x (CD15s) due to a mutation in the GDP-fucose transporter gene SLC35C1, leading to less severe infection susceptibility compared to LAD 1.
keyPoints: LAD 1: Persistent leukocytosis with resting neutrophil counts of 15,000–20,000/μL; recurrent bacterial infections involving skin, mucosa, and respiratory tract; delayed separation of the umbilical stump.
LAD 2: Less severe infection susceptibility than LAD 1; caused by abnormal fucosylation of SLe^x due to mutation in SLC35C1.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 3)

items.title: Clinical Features and Management of Myeloperoxidase Deficiency
items.content: Myeloperoxidase deficiency is a common neutrophil defect characterized by delayed but not absent microbicidal activity. It is inherited as an autosomal recessive trait with an incidence of ~1 in 2000 persons. While generally asymptomatic, patients with diabetes are more susceptible to Candida infections. Myeloperoxidase deficiency can also be acquired in conditions like myelomonocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia.
keyPoints: Incidence: ~1 in 2000 persons; inherited as an autosomal recessive trait.
Clinical significance: Delayed neutrophil microbicidal activity; increased susceptibility to Candida infections in patients with diabetes.
Acquired form: Seen in myelomonocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 3)

items.title: Understanding Chronic Granulomatous Disease (CGD) and its Genetic Basis
items.content: Chronic granulomatous disease (CGD) is a genetic disorder affecting granulocyte and monocyte oxidative metabolism due to defects in the enzyme NADPH oxidase, specifically NOX2. This results in impaired hydrogen peroxide production by leukocytes, leading to increased susceptibility to infections from specific microorganisms.
keyPoints: Incidence of CGD is ~1 in 100,000–200,000 individuals.
Two-thirds of cases are X-linked recessive; the rest are autosomal recessive.
Mutations affect genes encoding proteins for NOX2 assembly: a 91-kDa protein (X-linked), a 22-kDa protein (autosomal), and three cytoplasmic proteins (40, 47, 67 kDa).
Common infections in CGD patients include S. aureus, Serratia marcescens, Burkholderia cepacia complex, Nocardia, Aspergillus species, Salmonella, tuberculosis, and BCG outside North America.
Patients often experience extensive inflammatory reactions and granuloma formation due to impaired regulation of inflammation.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 4)

items.title: Phagocyte Activation Disorders Linked to NF-κB Pathway Defects
items.content: Mutations affecting the NF-κB signaling pathway can lead to various infection susceptibility syndromes. Depending on where these mutations occur, they may result in different clinical presentations, from ectodermal dysplasia and severe immune deficiency to pyogenic infections with blunted inflammatory responses.
keyPoints: Defects at a late stage of NF-κB signaling (NEMO) cause ectodermal dysplasia and severe immune deficiency.
Mutations in proteins interacting with NEMO can mimic NEMO deficiency phenotypes.
Defects closer to cell-surface receptors, such as in IRAK4 or MyD88, lead to susceptibility to pyogenic infections with reduced febrile responses early in life.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 4)

items.title: RAC2 Mutations and Their Impact on Phagocyte Function
items.content: Mutations in the RAC2 gene, which is involved in actin assembly, intracellular signaling, and superoxide production, can lead to various clinical manifestations. These range from early-onset sepsis with constitutively active mutations to abscess formation with dominant negative mutations, and common variable immunodeficiency with hyperactive mutations.
keyPoints: RAC2 is a small Rho-family GTPase involved in actin assembly and superoxide production.
Constitutively active RAC2 mutations cause early-onset sepsis.
Dominant negative mutations result in abscesses with impaired superoxide production.
Hyperactive mutations are associated with common variable immunodeficiency.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 4)

items.title: Role of TNF-α in Inflammatory and Catabolic Processes
items.content: Tumor Necrosis Factor-alpha (TNF-α) is a critical cytokine secreted by macrophages, playing significant roles in inflammation and catabolism. It acts as a pyrogen similar to IL-1, contributing to fever induction and the pathogenesis of gram-negative shock. TNF-α stimulates the production of reactive oxygen species by macrophages and neutrophils, which are crucial for microbial killing. Additionally, it induces catabolic changes leading to cachexia in chronic diseases.
keyPoints: TNF-α duplicates many actions of IL-1.
Plays a key role in gram-negative shock pathogenesis.
Stimulates production of hydrogen peroxide and toxic oxygen species by macrophages and neutrophils.
Induces catabolic changes contributing to cachexia.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTES

items.title: Macrophage Function in Intracellular Microbe Elimination
items.content: Macrophages are essential effector cells for eliminating intracellular microorganisms. They can fuse to form giant cells and granulomas, a process controlled by Interferon-gamma (IFN-γ), which is crucial for combating intracellular microbes like Mycobacterium tuberculosis and Leishmania. IFN-γ-induced nitric oxide production is an important mechanism against these parasites.
keyPoints: Macrophages fuse to form giant cells and granulomas in response to inflammatory stimuli.
IFN-γ controls the formation of granulomas, aiding in intracellular microbe elimination.
Nitric oxide induced by IFN-γ is vital against intracellular parasites like tuberculosis and Leishmania.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTES

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM
items.title: Monocytosis: Causes and Associations
items.content: Monocytosis is an increase in monocyte count, which can be associated with various infectious diseases, malignancies, and inflammatory conditions. It is important to recognize the underlying causes as they guide diagnosis and management.
keyPoints: Monocytosis is linked to tuberculosis, brucellosis, subacute bacterial endocarditis, Rocky Mountain spotted fever, malaria, visceral leishmaniasis (kala-azar), malignancies, leukemias, myeloproliferative syndromes, hemolytic anemias, chronic idiopathic neutropenias, and granulomatous diseases like sarcoidosis and Crohn disease.
Monocytosis can also be seen in certain collagen vascular diseases.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM
items.title: Impaired Monocyte Function and Autoinflammatory Diseases
items.content: Defects in mononuclear phagocyte function can result from genetic mutations, autoantibodies, or viral infections. These defects lead to impaired immune responses and are associated with various infectious and inflammatory syndromes.
keyPoints: Genetic defects in IFN-γ and IL-12 pathways impair intracellular killing of bacteria and viruses.
Autoantibodies against IFN-γ, IL-23, and GM-CSF can block macrophage functions, leading to infections with mycobacteria, fungi, and Nocardia.
Viral infections like influenza can cause abnormal monocyte chemotaxis.
HIV infects mononuclear phagocytes via CCR5 co-receptor.
Autoinflammatory diseases involve cytokine dysregulation; examples include TRAPs due to TNF-α receptor mutations and familial Mediterranean fever from PYRIN mutations.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM

items.title: Role of Eosinophils in Parasitic Infections
items.content: Eosinophils play a central role in host defense against invasive helminthic infections and have recently been recognized as important in the response to tuberculosis. Unlike neutrophils, eosinophils can recirculate and are longer-lived.
keyPoints: Eosinophils respond to eotaxin via specific chemoattractant receptors.
Central role in defense against hookworm, schistosomiasis, strongyloidiasis, toxocariasis, trichinosis, filariasis, echinococcosis, cysticercosis, and tuberculosis.
Longer-lived than neutrophils; can recirculate in tissues.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILS

items.title: Eosinophil Functionality and Inflammatory Response
items.content: Eosinophils contain granules with major basic protein, eosinophil peroxidase, cationic proteins, and ribonucleases that contribute to host defense against parasites and viruses. They also play a role in inflammation through interactions with mast cells.
keyPoints: Granules contain major basic protein (histaminase activity) important for parasite defense.
Eosinophil peroxidase catalyzes oxidation of substances by hydrogen peroxide, aiding microorganism killing.
Cationic proteins reduce heparin's anticoagulant activity; ribonucleases kill respiratory syncytial virus.
Charcot-Leyden crystal protein detoxifies lysophospholipids.
T cell-derived factors and mast cell-derived ECFa enhance eosinophil parasite killing.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILS

items.title: Understanding Etiologies and Management of Eosinophilia
items.content: Eosinophilia, defined as >500 eosinophils per μL of blood, can arise from various causes including drug allergies, collagen vascular diseases, malignancies, infections, and genetic disorders. It is crucial to identify the underlying cause for appropriate management.
keyPoints: Eosinophilia: >500 eosinophils/μL
Common causes: Drug allergies (e.g., iodides, aspirin), collagen vascular diseases, malignancies, helminthic infections, genetic disorders (e.g., Job's syndrome)
Idiopathic hypereosinophilic syndromes involve prolonged eosinophilia with organ dysfunction, notably heart and CNS
Severe cases: Eosinophil count 50,000–100,000/μL; may respond to imatinib if due to platelet-derived growth factor receptor mutations
Eosinophilia-myalgia syndrome: Characterized by eosinophilia >1000/μL and myalgias, often linked to L-tryptophan contamination
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILIA

items.title: Management Strategies for Hypereosinophilic Syndromes
items.content: In hypereosinophilic syndromes, organ damage is mediated by eosinophil-derived proteins. Management includes targeting IL-5 with monoclonal antibodies or using tyrosine kinase inhibitors like imatinib in specific genetic mutations.
keyPoints: Organ damage from eosinophil proteins (e.g., major basic protein)
Severe complications: Heart and CNS involvement
Treatment options: Glucocorticoids, hydroxyurea, IFN-α, monoclonal antibodies against IL-5 or its receptor
Imatinib for cases with platelet-derived growth factor receptor mutations
Eosinophilia-myalgia syndrome treatment: Withdraw L-tryptophan, use glucocorticoids
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILIA

items.title: Understanding the Mechanism of EOSINOPENIA in Acute Bacterial Infection
items.content: Eosinopenia occurs during acute bacterial infections and is not dependent on endogenous glucocorticoids, as it can occur even after total adrenalectomy. The mechanism remains unknown.
keyPoints: Occurs with stress such as acute bacterial infection.
Also seen post-treatment with glucocorticoids.
Independent of endogenous glucocorticoids (occurs in animals after total adrenalectomy).
No known adverse effects.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPENIA | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULIN E-RECURRENT INFECTION SYNDROME

items.title: Characteristics and Implications of Hyperimmunoglobulin E-Recurrent Infection Syndrome (Job's Syndrome)
items.content: Job's syndrome is a rare multisystem disease caused by autosomal dominant negative mutations in STAT3, leading to impaired immune responses. Patients exhibit characteristic facies, recurrent infections with minimal inflammation, and other systemic issues.
keyPoints: Caused by autosomal dominant negative mutations in STAT3.
Characteristic features include broad nose, kyphoscoliosis, eczema, and non-deciduating primary teeth.
Recurrent sinopulmonary and cutaneous infections with minimal inflammation (cold abscesses).
Pneumonias often cavitate leading to pneumatoceles.
Common complications include coronary artery aneurysms and cerebral demyelinated plaques.
Reduced IL-17-producing T cells, despite very high IgE levels.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPENIA | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULIN E-RECURRENT INFECTION SYNDROME

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Chronic Granulomatous Disease (CGD)
items.content: CGD is diagnosed using the nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) dye test or the dihydrorhodamine (DHR) oxidation test. The DHR test, though very sensitive for CGD, may show impaired responses in myeloperoxidase deficiency and acetaminophen ingestion.
keyPoints: CGD is detected with NBT dye test or DHR oxidation test.
DHR test is highly sensitive but can be affected by myeloperoxidase deficiency and acetaminophen ingestion.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT

items.title: Management Strategies for CGD Patients
items.content: Prophylactic antibiotics (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) and antifungals (itraconazole) significantly reduce life-threatening infections in CGD patients. Recombinant human IFN-γ, administered at 50 µg/m² subcutaneously three times weekly, reduces infection frequency by 70%.
keyPoints: Prophylactic antibiotics and antifungals lower infection rates in CGD.
Recombinant human IFN-γ dose: 50 µg/m² subcutaneously three times weekly.
IFN-γ reduces infections by 70% in CGD patients.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT

items.title: Prophylactic Antibiotic Use in Neutropenia
items.content: Patients with chronic or cyclic neutrophil counts <500/µL may benefit from prophylactic antibiotics and G-CSF. Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (160/800 mg) twice daily can prevent infections without increasing fungal infection rates.
keyPoints: Prophylactic antibiotics recommended for neutrophil counts <500/µL.
Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole dose: 160/800 mg twice daily.
No increased fungal infections with this regimen in CGD.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which leukocyte type is primarily responsible for the destruction of bacteria?
items.explanation: Neutrophils are classically thought to be critical to host defense against bacteria.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes

items.question: What is the term used for benignly low granulocyte counts in certain African-American ethnic groups?
items.explanation: Lower granulocyte numbers in African Americans are termed benign ethnic neutropenia.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes

items.question: Which gene's null expression is associated with lower granulocyte numbers in certain populations?
items.explanation: The lower number of granulocytes is associated with null expression of the Duffy antigen receptor for cytokines (DARC) gene.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes

items.question: Which cells produce cytokines and colony-stimulating factors (CSFs) required for the growth of monocytes and neutrophils?
options: Human blood monocytes, tissue macrophages, and stromal cells
items.explanation: Human blood monocytes, tissue macrophages, and stromal cells produce CSFs required for the growth of monocytes and neutrophils.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MATURATION

items.question: What is the term used to describe an increase in the number of blood neutrophils?
items.explanation: An increase in the number of blood neutrophils is called neutrophilia.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MATURATION

items.question: What do primary granules contain that is important for killing gram-negative bacteria?
items.explanation: Primary granules contain bactericidal/permeability-increasing protein, which is important for killing gram-negative bacteria.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MATURATION

items.question: What characterizes the nuclei of mature neutrophils?
options: They are bilobed and indicative of Pelger-Huet anomaly.
They contain up to four segments.
They have more than five nuclear lobes in normal conditions.
items.explanation: The nucleus of neutrophils normally contains up to four segments; excessive segmentation (>5) may indicate certain deficiencies or syndromes.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MATURATION

items.question: Which of the following molecules is primarily responsible for the 'rolling' of neutrophils along the endothelial surface?
items.explanation: Selectins, including E-selectin and P-selectin, mediate low-affinity interactions that result in the rolling of neutrophils along the endothelial surface.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary function of integrins on neutrophils during inflammation?
items.explanation: Integrins increase neutrophil adhesiveness, allowing them to stick to the endothelium through interactions with specific endothelial receptors.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a mediator involved in increasing blood flow and permeability during inflammation?
items.explanation: IL-8 is a chemotactic factor, not directly involved in mediating increased vasodilation or permeability.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 1)

items.question: Where do most neutrophils leave the body under normal conditions?
items.explanation: Most neutrophils exit the body through migration via mucous membranes of the oral cavity and especially the gastrointestinal tract.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 1)

items.question: Which enzyme is responsible for generating hypochlorous acid in neutrophils?
items.explanation: Neutrophil myeloperoxidase, along with hydrogen peroxide and chloride, generates hypochlorous acid.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 2)

items.question: What is the role of lactoferrin in neutrophils?
options: It catalyzes the reduction of oxygen to superoxide anion.
It chelates iron, inhibiting microbial growth.
items.explanation: Lactoferrin chelates iron, which is important for the growth of microorganisms, especially fungi.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 2)

items.question: What type of chemokine mainly attracts neutrophils?
items.explanation: CXC cytokines, such as IL-8, primarily attract neutrophils.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 2)

items.question: What clinical manifestations suggest a phagocytic cell disorder in patients with congenital phagocyte defects?
options: Recurrent, severe bacterial and fungal infections
Aphthous ulcers of mucous membranes, gingivitis, and periodontal disease
Skin, ear, upper and lower respiratory tract, and bone infections
items.explanation: Aphthous ulcers of mucous membranes (gray ulcers without pus) and gingivitis and periodontal disease suggest a phagocytic cell disorder.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.question: At what absolute neutrophil count (ANC) level is the control of endogenous microbial flora impaired?
items.explanation: When the ANC falls to <500 cells/μL, control of endogenous microbial flora is impaired.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.question: What is a common cause of iatrogenic neutropenia?
options: Use of cytotoxic or immunosuppressive therapies
items.explanation: The most common neutropenias are iatrogenic, resulting from the use of cytotoxic or immunosuppressive therapies for malignancy or control of autoimmune disorders.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.question: Which enzyme's hypofunctional polymorphisms can lead to marrow toxicity when using azathioprine and 6-mercaptopurine?
items.explanation: Hypofunctional polymorphisms in thiopurine methyltransferase can lead to accumulation of 6-thioguanine and profound marrow toxicity.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.question: Which gene mutation is associated with Kostmann's syndrome?
items.explanation: Kostmann's syndrome is due to mutations in the anti-apoptosis gene HAX-1.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.question: What condition is characterized by neutrophil hypersegmentation and bone marrow myeloid arrest?
items.explanation: The WHIM syndrome is characterized by neutrophil hypersegmentation and bone marrow myeloid arrest.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following syndromes involves mutations in the gene encoding mitochondrial RNA-processing endoribonuclease RMRP?
items.explanation: The cartilage-hair hypoplasia syndrome is due to mutations in the mitochondrial RNA-processing endoribonuclease RMRP.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.question: Which of these conditions results from a defect in neutrophil adherence due to CD18 mutation?
items.explanation: Leukocyte adhesion deficiency type 1 (LAD 1) results from a defect in neutrophil adherence due to CD18 mutation.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of LAD 1?
options: Persistent leukocytosis due to failure of neutrophils to marginate
items.explanation: LAD 1 is characterized by persistent leukocytosis because neutrophils do not marginate.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 3)

items.question: What is the underlying cause of LAD 2?
options: Defects in lysosomal transport protein LYST
items.explanation: LAD 2 is caused by an abnormality of fucosylation of SLe^x, affecting neutrophil rolling along the endothelium.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 3)

items.question: Which condition involves impaired chemotaxis and abnormal rates of microbial killing due to slow fusion of lysosomal granules with phagosomes?
items.explanation: Chédiak-Higashi syndrome involves impaired chemotaxis and abnormal rates of microbial killing due to slow fusion of lysosomal granules with phagosomes.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 3)

items.question: Which enzyme defect is responsible for chronic granulomatous disease (CGD)?
options: Defect in the enzyme NADPH oxidase, also called NOX2
Mutation in the gene encoding myeloperoxidase
items.explanation: CGD is due to defects in NADPH oxidase (NOX2), affecting phagocyte hydrogen peroxide production.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 4)

items.question: Which pathogen is NOT commonly associated with infections in patients with CGD in North America?
items.explanation: In North America, Salmonella is not commonly associated with CGD infections.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 4)

items.question: What role does the protein EROS play in chronic granulomatous disease?
options: It transports the 91- and 22-kDa proteins to the endoplasmic reticulum
It forms the heterodimer cytochrome b-558
It is responsible for cytokine synthesis
It interacts with Toll-like receptor signals
items.explanation: EROS transports the 91- and 22-kDa proteins to the endoplasmic reticulum, crucial in CGD.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 4)

items.question: Which of the following is a late complication increasingly recognized in adolescent and adult patients with CGD?
items.explanation: Late complications in CGD include nodular regenerative hyperplasia and portal hypertension.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 4)

items.question: Which cells in the mononuclear phagocyte system have a half-life of 12–24 hours?
items.explanation: Monocytes leave circulation by diapedesis more slowly than neutrophils and have a half-life of 12–24 hours.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTES

items.question: Which cytokine secreted by macrophages is involved in initiating fever in the hypothalamus?
items.explanation: IL-1 has functions including initiating fever in the hypothalamus.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTES

items.question: What is a key role of macrophages in response to certain inflammatory stimuli?
items.explanation: Macrophages can fuse to form giant cells that coalesce into granulomas in response to some inflammatory stimuli.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTES

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM
items.question: Which of the following conditions is associated with monocytosis?
options: HIV infection without disseminated nontuberculous mycobacterial infection
items.explanation: Monocytosis is associated with tuberculosis, among other conditions.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM
items.question: What can cause impaired monocyte cytokine production or response in patients without HIV?
items.explanation: Impaired monocyte cytokine production or response can occur in patients with disseminated nontuberculous mycobacterial infections who are not infected with HIV.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM
items.question: Which viral infection is known to impair mononuclear phagocyte function by causing abnormal chemotaxis?
items.explanation: Influenza virus infection causes abnormal monocyte chemotaxis, impairing mononuclear phagocyte function.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM

items.question: Which chemokine specifically attracts eosinophils?
items.explanation: Eosinophils respond to the specific chemokine eotaxin.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILS

items.question: What is a distinctive feature of eosinophil granules when stained with Wright's stain?
options: crystalline core consisting of an arginine-rich protein
items.explanation: The distinctive feature is the crystalline core consisting of an arginine-rich protein.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILS

items.question: Which condition is eosinophils NOT typically important in?
items.explanation: Eosinophils appear unimportant during most bacterial infections.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILS

items.question: Which cytokine is the dominant growth factor for eosinophils?
items.explanation: IL-5 is identified as the dominant eosinophil growth factor.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILIA

items.question: Which condition is characterized by eosinophilia and generalized disabling myalgias without other recognized causes?
items.explanation: The eosinophilia-myalgia syndrome is characterized by eosinophilia and generalized disabling myalgias.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILIA

items.question: Which therapeutic approach can specifically inhibit the dominant eosinophil growth factor?
options: Monoclonal antibodies against IL-5 or its receptor
items.explanation: IL-5 can be specifically inhibited with monoclonal antibodies against it or its receptor.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILIA

items.question: Which of the following is a known cause of eosinopenia?
items.explanation: Eosinopenia occurs with stress, such as acute bacterial infection.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPENIA | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULIN E-RECURRENT INFECTION SYNDROME

items.question: What is the mechanism of eosinopenia in acute bacterial infection?
options: Caused by increased IL-17-producing T cells
items.explanation: The mechanism is independent of endogenous glucocorticoids, as it occurs after total adrenalectomy.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPENIA | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULIN E-RECURRENT INFECTION SYNDROME

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of Job's syndrome?
options: High levels of IL-17-producing T cells
Cold abscesses due to reduced inflammation
Severe allergy with high IgE levels
items.explanation: Job's syndrome is characterized by 'cold abscesses' due to much less inflammation than appropriate for the degree of infection.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPENIA | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULIN E-RECURRENT INFECTION SYNDROME

items.question: Which test is used to detect Chronic Granulomatous Disease (CGD) based on the ability of products of oxidative metabolism to alter oxidation states of reporter molecules?
items.explanation: The NBT dye test is used to detect CGD by assessing oxidative metabolism.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT

items.question: What is a recommended prophylactic treatment for patients with Chronic Granulomatous Disease (CGD) to reduce the frequency of life-threatening infections?
items.explanation: Prophylactic antibiotics (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) and antifungals are recommended for CGD.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which test is used to assess in vivo the ability of leukocytes and inflammatory mediators to accumulate locally in the skin?
items.explanation: The Rebuck skin window test measures leukocyte and inflammatory mediator accumulation.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT

items.question: What is the recommended dose of recombinant human IFN-γ for reducing infection frequency in patients with CGD?
items.explanation: The recommended dose is 50 µg/m² subcutaneously three times weekly.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT

items.statement: Benign ethnic neutropenia in African Americans usually leads to significant health problems.
items.explanation: The condition is generally without sequelae.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes

items.statement: Neutrophils are derived from a common stem cell in the bone marrow along with other leukocytes.
items.explanation: All leukocytes, including neutrophils, are derived from a common stem cell in the bone marrow.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes

items.statement: Neutrophil heterogeneity is well understood and its implications are clear.
items.explanation: The meaning of neutrophil heterogeneity is not known.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MATURATION

items.statement: Toxic granulations in neutrophils are mature azurophil granules.
items.explanation: Toxic granulations are immature or abnormally staining azurophil granules.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MATURATION

items.statement: Neutrophil margination in the lung is primarily mediated by selectins.
items.explanation: In the lungs, neutrophil margination occurs due to size constraints and fluidity requirements rather than selectin-mediated interactions.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 1)

items.statement: PECAM-1 is involved in the diapedesis of neutrophils.
items.explanation: PECAM-1, expressed on both leukocytes and endothelial cells, facilitates the migration of neutrophils through endothelial junctions during diapedesis.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 1)

items.statement: Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) are composed of a DNA scaffold with proteins that help in microbial killing.
items.explanation: NETs consist of a DNA scaffold decorated with neutrophil-granule derived proteins, aiding in immobilizing and killing microorganisms.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 2)

items.statement: Myeloperoxidase imparts the characteristic red color to pus.
items.explanation: Myeloperoxidase confers the green color to pus, not red.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 2)

items.statement: Drug-induced neutropenia is irreversible even after discontinuation of the sensitizing drug.
items.explanation: Discontinuation of the sensitizing drug usually results in recovery within 5–7 days, and it is complete by 10 days.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.statement: Acquired neutropenia may be cyclic and associated with an expansion of large granular lymphocytes.
items.explanation: Acquired neutropenia can be cyclic in nature, occurring at intervals of several weeks, and may be associated with an expansion of large granular lymphocytes.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.statement: Mutations in the G-CSF receptor are associated with severe congenital neutropenia and can lead to leukemia.
items.explanation: Severe congenital neutropenia linked to mutations in the G-CSF receptor is indeed associated with an increased risk of developing leukemia.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.statement: In Felty syndrome, splenectomy can decrease serum neutrophil-binding IgG levels.
items.explanation: Splenectomy in Felty syndrome may increase the neutrophil count and lower serum neutrophil-binding IgG.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.statement: Myeloperoxidase deficiency is associated with clinically compromised defenses in all individuals.
items.explanation: Myeloperoxidase deficiency does not compromise defenses unless there is another factor, such as diabetes.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 3)

items.statement: Specific granule deficiency can be caused by mutations in CEBPE.
items.explanation: Specific granule deficiency is associated with mutations in the gene CEBPE.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 3)

items.statement: Patients with chronic granulomatous disease are protected from atherosclerosis.
items.explanation: CGD patients have been reported to be protected from atherosclerosis, implicating NOX2 in its pathogenesis.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 4)

items.statement: Mutations affecting NF-κB signal transduction always result in ectodermal dysplasia.
items.explanation: Ectodermal dysplasia occurs with defects at a late stage of NF-κB activation, not all mutations.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 4)

items.statement: Monocytes are derived from the yolk sac during embryonic development.
items.explanation: Many tissue macrophages arise in the embryonic yolk sac, not monocytes.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTES

items.statement: Macrophage elastase is involved in the development of emphysema from cigarette smoking.
items.explanation: Macrophage elastase mediates the development of emphysema from cigarette smoking.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTES

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM
items.statement: Autoantibodies to IFN-γ can lead to mycobacterial infections.
items.explanation: Autoantibodies to IFN-γ block critical signals for macrophage intracellular killing, leading to mycobacterial infections.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM
items.statement: Monocytopenia is commonly seen in patients with sarcoidosis.
items.explanation: Sarcoidosis is associated with monocytosis, not monocytopenia.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · DISORDERS OF THE MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTE SYSTEM

items.statement: Eosinophil peroxidase can initiate mast cell secretion in vitro.
items.explanation: In the presence of hydrogen peroxide and halide, eosinophil peroxidase initiates mast cell secretion.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILS

items.statement: Eosinophils are primarily involved in host defense against bacterial infections.
items.explanation: Eosinophils play a central role in host defense during invasive helminthic infections, not typically bacterial.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILS

items.statement: Eosinophilia is commonly present in helminthic infections.
items.explanation: The passage states that eosinophilia is commonly present in helminthic infections.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILIA

items.statement: Idiopathic hypereosinophilic syndromes are caused by known mutations.
items.explanation: The idiopathic hypereosinophilic syndromes have unknown causes, although some cases involve specific mutations.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPHILIA

items.statement: Eosinopenia has known adverse effects.
items.explanation: There is no known adverse effect of eosinopenia.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPENIA | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULIN E-RECURRENT INFECTION SYNDROME

items.statement: Patients with Job's syndrome have reduced IL-17-producing T cells.
items.explanation: IL-17-producing T cells, responsible for protection against extracellular and mucosal infections, are reduced in Job's syndrome.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPENIA | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULIN E-RECURRENT INFECTION SYNDROME

items.statement: Patients with CGD should avoid TNF-α-blocking agents due to increased susceptibility to infection.
items.explanation: TNF-α-blocking agents increase susceptibility to infection in CGD patients.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT

items.statement: Prophylactic antibiotics are necessary for all patients with chronic neutropenia regardless of bone marrow reserve.
items.explanation: Patients with good bone marrow reserves do not need prophylactic antibiotics.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT

items.assertion: The Duffy antigen receptor for cytokines (DARC) gene's null expression is linked to resistance against Plasmodium vivax malaria.
items.reason: The absence of the DARC gene, which serves as a receptor for Plasmodium vivax, confers resistance to this form of malaria.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes

items.assertion: Excessive segmentation of neutrophil nuclei (>5 nuclear lobes) can indicate folate deficiency.
items.reason: Folate or vitamin B12 deficiency may manifest as excessive segmentation in neutrophils.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MATURATION

items.assertion: The Pelger-Huet anomaly results from mutations in the lamin B receptor.
items.reason: Pelger-Huet anomaly causes neutrophils with distinctive bilobed nuclei.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MATURATION

items.assertion: Increased neutrophil rigidity leads to decreased margination in the lung.
items.reason: Increased neutrophil rigidity and decreased deformability lead to augmented trapping and margination in the pulmonary circulation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; increased rigidity actually enhances margination due to difficulty passing through capillaries.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 1)

items.assertion: Neutrophils are primarily cleared from the circulation by macrophages in the spleen.
items.reason: Senescent neutrophils are cleared from the circulation by macrophages in both the lung and spleen.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but it omits that clearance also occurs significantly in the lungs.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MARROW RELEASE AND CIRCULATING COMPARTMENTS (part 1)

items.assertion: Acute neutropenia is more likely to be associated with increased risk of infection than chronic neutropenia.
items.reason: Acute neutropenia, such as that caused by cancer chemotherapy, presents a higher immediate risk compared to chronic neutropenia which reverses in response to infection or controlled administration of endotoxin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why acute neutropenia is more risky.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.assertion: Pharmacogenomic testing is recommended before initiating azathioprine or 6-mercaptopurine to prevent marrow toxicity.
items.reason: Hypofunctional polymorphisms in thiopurine methyltransferase can lead to accumulation of toxic metabolites and profound marrow toxicity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why pharmacogenomic testing is recommended.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.assertion: Neutrophilia can result from increased production, marrow release, or defective margination.
items.reason: The most important acute cause of neutrophilia is infection, which involves both increased production and marrow release.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why neutrophilia can result from these factors.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.assertion: Leukocyte adhesion deficiency type 1 results in poor adherence of neutrophils to endothelial cells.
items.reason: LAD 1 is caused by mutations in the CD18 gene, which affects integrin function necessary for tight adhesion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why LAD 1 results in poor adherence.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 2)

items.assertion: Patients with CGD have increased susceptibility to infections.
items.reason: Leukocytes in CGD patients have severely diminished hydrogen peroxide production, impairing bacterial killing.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; impaired hydrogen peroxide production leads to increased infection risk.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 4)

items.assertion: Mutations in RAC2 can lead to common variable immunodeficiency.
items.reason: RAC2 mutations result in hyperactive forms that impair superoxide production.
items.explanation: Both are true, but the reason is incorrect; hyperactive RAC2 mutations cause common variable immunodeficiency.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · NEUTROPHIL ABNORMALITIES (part 4)

items.assertion: Macrophages are key effector cells in eliminating intracellular microorganisms.
items.reason: Their ability to form giant cells and granulomas is under the control of IFN-γ.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains how macrophages eliminate intracellular microbes.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · MONONUCLEAR PHAGOCYTES

items.assertion: Job's syndrome is caused by autosomal dominant negative mutations in STAT3.
items.reason: These mutations impair normal STAT signaling with broad and profound effects.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · EOSINOPENIA | HYPERIMMUNOGLOBULIN E-RECURRENT INFECTION SYNDROME

items.assertion: Recombinant human IFN-γ reduces the frequency and severity of infections in CGD patients.
items.reason: IFN-γ nonspecifically stimulates phagocytic cell function, reducing infection rates by 70%.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true; IFN-γ's stimulation of phagocytes explains its effect.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT

items.assertion: Rigorous oral hygiene completely eliminates the discomfort from gingivitis in patients with neutrophil disorders.
items.reason: Chlorhexidine mouthwash and tooth brushing help reduce discomfort but do not eliminate it.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; rigorous oral hygiene reduces but does not completely eliminate discomfort.
items.reference: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS AND MANAGEMENT
